evidence: We rely on artificial intelligence (AI) technologies to support and enhance various aspects of our products, services, and internal operations.
If we are unable to effectively implement, monitor, and manage these technologies, our business, financial condition, and results of operations could be adversely affected.
We rely on artificial intelligence (AI) technologies to support and enhance various aspects of our products, services, and internal operations.
artificial intelligence shopping agents may autonomously recommend products that are not ours and, in some cases, directly compete with our products.

AI models can generate inaccurate, biased, or unpredictable outputs, and failures in data quality, system design, or oversight could result in operational disruptions, security or privacy incidents, reputational challenges.

changes in the way AI is regulated including potential new requirements governing transparency, accountability, data usage, and model controls could increase our compliance costs, limit the use of certain technologies.
Any failure to comply with emerging AI regulatory frameworks could result in enforcement actions, fines, or other adverse consequences.

limitations: ai_investment_intensity: No named AI systems, vendors, partners, dollar amounts, or dedicated budgets disclosed in excerpts; only generic statements about relying on AI technologies.

ai_narrative_concreteness: AI use cases referenced only at a high level (operations, products, services) with no deployment status, named initiatives, or metrics; language is largely risk-framing rather than operational detail.

ai_risk_disclosure_depth: Excerpts are from the business/risk-factor section and contain company-specific risk mechanisms (operational disruptions, compliance costs, enforcement actions) tied to AI use and regulation, supporting a score of 4; however, no quantified risk exposure or named governance/oversight bodies are disclosed, precluding a score of 5.
